thread.title: Prison project could risk work release program and some medical beds under potential bidding plan | Local News | lancasteronline.com
thread.title_full: Prison project could risk work release program and some medical beds under potential bidding plan | Local News | lancasteronline.com

author: SARAH NICELL snicell@lnpnews.com
title: Prison project could risk work release program and some medical beds under potential bidding plan | Local News | lancasteronline.com
text: Lancaster County Prison Warden Cheryl Steberger on Thursday presented a rough plan for the new prison project that was short on details but intended to give the commissioners options for reducing costs. The new plan, if approved, would prioritize what the warden and her colleagues consider the prison’s core necessities when putting out the project for bids, while allowing builders to make their pitches for optional add-ins. The core design features in the new prison would include sleeping areas, restrooms and recreational spaces. Other features would go to bid separately. An example of one change relates to beds in the prison’s medical section. The core bid envisions 20 hospital beds, but the optional bid would expand that number to 27 beds. Construction companies bidding to build the prison would not be required to bid on the optional features, and commissioners could address the add-ins on a case-by-case basis. “Of course, I would not like to have to do alternatives, but I also have a responsibility to the taxpayers, to the community, to do what’s right here,” Steberger said at a meeting of the county prison board. In August, building the new prison was projected to cost up to $407 million . The prison board has not publicly discussed how much the new bidding plan could save the county, or how much square footage could be removed from the design. The spaces listed as bid alternates for the new prison are not insignificant and include support group areas, a maintenance space for facilities management storage and the central booking space. All could be dropped as requirements under the proposed bidding format. The current prison has a work release area that serves inmates who are working toward reentry into society by taking jobs during the day. If the proposed bidding process is approved and the work release area is not picked up as part of the prison design, the program could be significantly changed or scaled back, according to Steberger. “Work release is a real stickler for me. … It’s one of those alternates I probably don’t feel comfortable with (losing),” Steberger said. More changes expected The commissioners likely won’t make any decisions on bidding for at least another few months, according to Republican Commissioner Ray D’Agostino. Even so, D’Agostino said Thursday he would support the alternative bid format, but he doesn’t want a new plan to change the schematic design the commissioners have spent months reviewing. “I am very seriously concerned if we’re going to build a new prison, we don’t build simply a newer facility that is essentially like the old facility,” D’Agostino said. Bob Devonshire, the Lancaster County facilities management director, said the August schematic created by Kansas City company TranSystems likely would remain the same under the new proposal, aside from markings indicating the alternate sections of the facility. “It’s still the same big picture. It’s just portions of it have the potential of not being there,” Devonshire said. Parts of the new prison labeled as bid alternates will still likely end up in the design, at least eventually, Steberger said. “If a particular building element is not shown as part of the base bid, that does not mean that the element will not be built,” she said. “It means that the element could be included if the add alternate is accepted. It could be added to the project down the road as a change order, or it could be added to the facility for future years through a standalone project.” While bid alternates are a common part of public bidding for expensive construction projects to help manage costs, they typically include only items the facility could survive without, said Gary Weaver, a founding partner with Tippetts/Weaver Architects on North Duke Street. Weaver also said people sometimes worry that bid alternates enable the facility owner, in this case the county, to skew which bidder is really cheapest by picking and choosing alternates to keep or abandon. “I think there’s also concerns about the alternate process because it gives the owner the option of being selective of what scopes of work they take, which also gives them potentially the ability to sway who was the low bidder,” he said. A reanalysis of needs by Steberger and colleagues this month also led to a request for 986 total beds in the new facility, eight less than the pitch at September’s board meeting. Changes to the project will continue for months as the commissioners try to strike a balance between inmate needs and financing.
